text:
Do you watch movies with subtitles? 
If you do, this video is for you.

I'm going to explain how you 
can improve your listening
so you don't have to rely on subtitles anymore.
I call it the No-Subtitles Method.

But before we dive in, I 
need to be honest with you.
This method doesn't work for everyone.

To see if it will work for 
you, let's do a simple test.

Here's what you do: pick a 
movie you've never seen before,
turn on English subtitles, and start watching.
As you watch, keep reading the subtitles.

See if you can understand the 
text without looking up any words.

It's okay if you miss a few details, and 
you don't have to understand every sentence.

If you can follow the movie 
pretty easily and don't need
to pause to look up words, then congratulations!

Your English is good enough 
for the No-Subtitles Method.
Now, the method itself is very simple.

From now on, just start watching 
movies without subtitles.

That's it. No more text on 
the screen. No more reading.

If you want to understand the dialogue, 
you need to let your ears do all the work.

Now, when you remove the subtitles, 
it's going to be a bit of a struggle.

You'll feel a bit lost because you're 
only catching parts of the dialogue.
You'll recognize some words, but not all of them.

When that happens, don't 
panic. That's totally normal.

If you feel the urge to turn on the subtitles, 
fight that urge with everything you've got.

Don't take the easy way out. Just 
trust your ears and keep going.
Pretty soon, something amazing will happen.
You'll start to understand more and more.

The dialogue won't sound like 
noise or gibberish anymore.
It'll start to make a lot of sense.

And trust me, this will 
happen sooner than you think.

If you do this, you'll be amazed at how much 
your listening improves in a short time.
Now, you might find this hard to believe.
You might think, "This is stupid."

"Improving listening can't be that 
simple. This video is a waste of time."
And look, I understand that feeling.

After all, I don't even know you. 
I don't know your English level.

So how can I be so sure this 
method will work for you?

Well, there's a reason why 
I'm so confident about this.

The reason is simple: you 
already have a strong vocabulary.
Think about it: when you watch a movie,

you can understand the subtitles 
without looking up words, right?

That means you know enough words 
to follow and enjoy most movies.
So the problem isn't your vocabulary.
The problem—the real problem—is your ears.

Basically, you know enough words, but 
your ears don't recognize many of them.
That's why you need subtitles.
It's not because your vocabulary is too small.

It's just that your ears 
haven't had enough training.

So if you start training your ears the right 
way, your listening will definitely improve.
Now, let's talk about how to do that.

You see, a lot of people use subtitles 
when they practice listening.

And that's okay for people who are 
still building up their vocabulary.

But for someone like you, subtitles 
are actually holding you back.

Think about it. When subtitles are on, you 
have two ways to understand the dialogue:

you can either read the 
text or listen to the audio.

Now, because text is way easier for the brain 
to process, you'll naturally focus on that.
Look at the sentence on your screen right now.

You know exactly what it means, 
right? The text is crystal clear.
There's no mistake, no misunderstanding.
But if you hear that same sentence in a movie—with

music and background noise—you might 
hear something entirely different.

So the text is 100% reliable. 
The audio? Not so much.
That's why your brain loves reading text.
So, as long as those subtitles are on,

your brain isn't going to pay much 
attention to the spoken words.

Your ears will never get 
the full workout they need,

and your listening will get 
stuck at the same level forever.

So, if you really want to improve, you have 
to give your ears a reason to get better.

When you remove the subtitles, your brain 
has no choice but to focus on the audio.

Suddenly, your ears have to work 
really hard to understand the dialogue.
That's how you force your listening to level up.

Now listen, I understand that going from 
subtitles to nothing can feel a bit extreme.

So, to make things a little 
easier, you can start with YouTube.
Watch YouTube videos without subtitles first.

It's usually easier than movies because 
people tend to speak more clearly there.

Once you've built up enough 
confidence, you can move on to movies.

Pick a movie from your watchlist, switch off 
the subtitles, and get lost in the story.

Now that you don't need subtitles 
anymore, you can fully enjoy the show.
